plot_beats.beat_text: [mode:teacher vessel:the tea-house hands / wound] the cadet who can't rest forces the ritual — apparatus: an elder whose daily ritual IS the doctrine; learned by watching hands, not hearing words. Teacher/musician is NEVER named; the vessel carries the mode diegetically.

Open on the weather. the empty beach at low tide under a turning sky — first cold of the season, or the cicadas gone quiet, or rain that arrives earlier than expected and without apology. No people yet. Just the world, breathing.
Mira appears inside the weather, mid-habit — setting out two cups, opening the shutters, sweeping the step. The body moves before the mind wakes. We learn who they are by what their hands do.
A small wrongness. Mira sets out the second cup, then remembers, and puts one back. The absence has a shape. We never name it. The empty place at the table says everything the caption won't.

The disruption arrives the way weather arrives. the child enters — a stranger at the door, a letter on the step, a light that won't leave a window. They bring no threat. They bring a question Mira has been not-asking.
Mira's first instinct is to tidy the feeling away — to offer tea, to change the subject, to be useful. The old armor of usefulness. the child lets the silence sit instead.
They do something ordinary together — boil chestnuts, mend a net, walk to where the water is. The task is not the point. The being- beside is the point. The grief loosens its grip a quarter-turn.
A wordless stretch. Wind in the eaves. Steam off the cups. A leaf deciding. Mira stops performing okayness and simply is, for three panels, beside another person and the weather.

Mira says one true thing — small, halting, unforced. Not a confession of the whole grief. Just the size of it that fits in a single breath. "I keep setting out the second cup." the child does not fix it. They nod.
the child leaves the way weather leaves — without ceremony. Mira stands in the doorway. The light has changed. Nothing is resolved. Something has settled.

Return to baseline, one degree deeper. Mira sets out one cup now — on purpose, not from forgetting. They sit on the porch and watch the water change its mind about which stones to cover. The season turns. The reader exhales.

[mode:teacher vessel:the tea-house hands / turn] watching the hands, the body settles before the mind agrees — apparatus: an elder whose daily ritual IS the doctrine; learned by watching hands, not hearing words. Teacher/musician is NEVER named; the vessel carries the mode diegetically.

Open on weather and place. the empty beach at low tide, a season deep. Something in the light is fractionally wrong — a seam of gold where no lamp should be, hovering at the height of a child's eyes. The world is calm. The anomaly is calm too.
Mira — keeper, medium, shopkeeper, wanderer — notices it the way one notices a change in the air. No alarm. They have met presences before. This one is shaped like someone's missing.
the child — the one who lives with the presence — describes it wrong, the way the grieving describe their grief: "the light that won't leave her window." Mira listens for what is under the words.
Mira does not banish it. They approach it the way you approach a frightened animal or an old sorrow — slowly, sideways, without demand. The presence does not flee. It leans toward the warmth.
A wordless stretch with the presence. It is not threatening. It is lonely. The panels hold steam, dust motes, the gold seam pulsing like a slow heartbeat. Mira simply stays.

the child confesses the real thing — the grief the presence is made of. Not all of it. The breath-sized piece. "I never said goodbye." The presence brightens, gently, as if it has been waiting to be acknowledged.

The work is not to make the presence leave. The work is to let it be seen. Mira names what it is — softly, without certainty. "It came about the light that won't leave her window, not the child who won't sleep."
The presence loosens. It does not vanish in a flash; it thins like morning fog, the way grief doesn't end but learns to hold less of the light. A degree of it stays. It always will.
the child can sleep now — not because the grief is gone, but because it has been witnessed. Mira gathers their pack the way weather gathers to move on.

Return to baseline, one degree deeper, mystery intact. The last of the gold seam catches the morning. Mira walks the road out, small in the frame. What the presence truly was stays, kindly, unknowable.

[mode:teacher vessel:the tea-house hands / renewal] the ritual kept, lightly, on its own — apparatus: an elder whose daily ritual IS the doctrine; learned by watching hands, not hearing words. Teacher/musician is NEVER named; the vessel carries the mode diegetically.
Open on weather that returns. The same season, come around again. the empty beach at low tide wears a familiar light, and the light carries something Mira has spent a year not looking at directly.
Mira keeps busy. The rituals are a little too brisk today, the usefulness a little too useful. The body is managing a feeling the mind won't name.
A found object surfaces — a letter, a glove, a photograph in a drawer that wasn't being searched. Mira almost puts it back. Almost walks on. The whole chapter lives in that almost.
They set it on the table. They make the tea. They do not put the object away. The small courage is not a leap — it is the choice to stay in the room with the feeling for one more minute.
A wordless stretch. Mira and the object and the weather. The ache is allowed, at last, to simply be in the room, neither fed nor fled.

They do one small honoring thing — refold the letter gently, set the glove by the window, place the photograph upright instead of face- down. The gesture says what no caption should: I am not pretending you weren't here.
Mira says the breath-sized true thing — aloud, to the empty room, which is to say to themselves. "I miss this version of the morning." It is not the whole grief. It is the size that fits.
Nothing is fixed. The ache does not dissolve. But it has been crossed-toward instead of fled, and that changes its weight. Grief that is sat-with takes up less of the air than grief that is run-from.
Mira returns to the day, the object now belonging instead of haunting. The rituals slow to their right pace. The light moves on across the floor.
Return to baseline, one degree deeper. Mira on the porch, small in the frame, watching the season do what seasons do. They have crossed a small, real distance today. The reader feels it as their own.
